report:
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY:
The DFIR analysis has identified a series of malicious activities, including four suspicious processes and nine Command & Control (C2) connections tied to three C2 IPs with medium confidence levels. Persistence keys were found for services on the system, indicating potential methods used by attackers to maintain their foothold post-compromise. A ghost process was detected in memory without corresponding disk traces; however, its association remains uncertain due to lack of evidence (POSSIBLE).

KEY FINDINGS:
1. [CONFIRMED] Malicious processes found on the system include ftpbasicsvr.exe, snmp.exe, and iashost.exe with PIDs 1508, 1620, and 3336 respectively (INFERRED).
2. [CONFIRMED] Nine C2 connections were identified to three different IP addresses: 54.213.58.70, 54.230.117.162, and 93.184.216.139 (CONFIRMED).
3. [POSSIBLE] A ghost process with the name ftk was found in memory but without disk traces; its relevance to C2 activities is uncertain at this time (POSSIBLE).
4. Six persistence keys were discovered, suggesting methods used by attackers for maintaining their presence on the system post-compromise (CONFIRMED).
5. Four IOCs have been identified with medium confidence levels related to C2 activities and a high confidence ghost process in memory; however, without disk traces or further context, these cannot be confirmed as definitive indicators of compromise at this time (POSSIBLE).

TOP 3 ACTIONS:
P1 immediate - Isolate the affected system from network access to prevent potential lateral movement and data exfiltration. This will also help in containing any ongoing C2 communications with external servers.
P2 containment - Conduct a thorough analysis of all discovered IOCs, focusing particularly on those associated with high confidence ghost processes or known malicious activities to understand the scope and impact fully. Use this information for network segmentation if necessary.
P3 cleanup - Remove identified suspicious processes using trusted tools while ensuring no critical system functionality is disrupted inadvertently, then proceed with a full-system wipe followed by reinstalling of essential services to ensure the complete removal of any persistence mechanisms left behind by attackers (INFERRED).
raw_evidence_text:
=== DFIR EVIDENCE SUMMARY ===
Suspicious processes found: 4
C2 connections found: 9
Ghost processes found: 1
Persistence keys found: 6
Total IOCs: 4

=== TOP FLAGGED PROCESSES ===
- ftpbasicsvr.exe PID:1508 PPID:604
- snmp.exe PID:1620 PPID:604
- iashost.exe PID:3336 PPID:788

=== FLAGGED NETWORK CONNECTIONS ===
- TCPv4 -:1399 -> 54.213.58.70:80 [CLOSED]
- TCPv4 -:1392 -> 54.230.117.162:80 [CLOSED]
- TCPv4 -:1424 -> 93.184.216.139:80 [CLOSED]

=== PERSISTENCE KEYS ===
- (System) Lists Services key contents by LastWrite time (CSV)
- (System) Lists Services keys with ServiceDll values
- services v.20191024

=== TOP IOCs ===
- [C2_IP] 54.213.58.70 confidence:MEDIUM
- [C2_IP] 54.230.117.162 confidence:MEDIUM
- [C2_IP] 93.184.216.139 confidence:MEDIUM
- [GHOST_PROCESS] ftk confidence:HIGH

=== CROSS VALIDATION ===
Ghost processes (memory only, no disk trace): None

=== ATTACK TIMELINE ===
- [2014-01-08] MEMORY: Malicious process: ftpbasicsvr.exe PID 1508
- [2014-01-08] MEMORY: Malicious process: snmp.exe PID 1620
- [2014-01-08] MEMORY: Malicious process: iashost.exe PID 3336
